original_abstract: By prioritizing global image structure over pixel-level details, our approach enables generative modeling that is robust to spatial variations such as rotation and translation. This study presents a framework for high-resolution 3D image synthesis that leverages the latent space of a VQ-GAN to significantly reduce computational overhead. We demonstrate the versatility of our method across four diverse medical imaging datasets, even when constrained by limited sample sizes ($n \leq 1844$). Our model consistently outperforms established GAN benchmarks by producing greater diversity and exhibiting higher resilience to mode collapse. Recognizing that standard quantitative metrics often fail to capture the nuances of medical imaging, we incorporated expert evaluations from experienced radiologists to validate the clinical realism of our results. Finally, we highlight the utility of our synthetic data in low-resource environments: by using synthetic images for self-supervised pre-training of a Swin-UNETR, we achieved substantial performance gains in downstream segmentation tasks at external institutions. These findings underscore the potential of synthetic data to bridge the gap in medical imaging domains where high-quality labeled datasets are notoriously scarce.
humanized_abstract: Our recent paper on “Flexible High-Resolution 3D Image Synthesis Via Global Image Structure Modeling with VQ-GAN” was just accepted to MICCAI. This paper presents a flexible high-resolution 3D image synthesis framework that can generate diverse 3D medical images with robustness to arbitrary spatial transformations such as rotation and translation. The proposed framework can achieve efficient and fast 3D image synthesis while utilizing the latent space of VQ-GAN to speed up computations. Our proposed method can be extensively validated on a wide range of 3D medical image datasets in different anatomical locations, as our method can adapt to different datasets with only a few examples, shown as four 3D medical imaging datasets with small number of examples (n ≤ 1844), and it achieves better performance than several state-of-the-art GANs with higher diversity and less mode collapse. Moreover, due to the limitations of evaluation metrics for medical images, we have evaluated the quality of our generated images using clinical insights from radiologists, confirming the high clinical fidelity of the synthesized data. In addition, in the case of low resource settings, we demonstrated that our synthetic images can boost the performance of a state-of-the-art segmentation model, Swin-UNETR, trained in our collaborating institutions, which could benefit resource-limited hospitals in medical image analysis.

Our proposed method can be extensively validated on a wide range of 3D medical image datasets in different anatomical locations, as our method can adapt to different datasets with only a few examples, shown as four 3D medical imaging datasets with small number of examples (n ≤ 1844), and it achieves better performance than several state-of-the-art GANs with higher diversity and less mode collapse.
